narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put together a thoughtful application, including your middle-family-income background and early academic deciles in the lower range from school years. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated your probability of failing at 40%—a risk level that unfortunately tipped the decision toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out most strongly to the model. Your undergraduate GPA was the single biggest positive factor, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass because it's close to the typical 3.21 average and signals decent college-level preparation. However, your Year 3 school decile of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—emerged as the top concern, strongly nudging the model toward failure by highlighting relatively weaker performance later in your schooling compared to peers. Your middle family income also helped in a modest way, leaning toward a passing prediction since it aligns with many successful students' backgrounds. Overall, while your GPA provided a strong anchor, the dip in your later school deciles carried the most weight in outweighing the positives and driving the failure risk higher than for typical applicants, who often show steadier upward trends. Your LSAT score of 25, notably lower than the average 36.7, and lower Year 1 decile played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the average, strengthening your later academic showings through additional coursework or recommendations, and reapplying next cycle—many students in similar situations succeed by addressing these gaps head-on. You're clearly motivated, and with targeted improvements, a school like this could see you as a stronger bet for bar success. Keep building on that GPA foundation you've already got.
